elements.sent-2411-001: The random population control decision problem asks for the existence of a controller capable of gathering almost-surely a whole population of identical finite-state agents simultaneously in a final state.
elements.sent-2411-002: The controller must be able to satisfy this requirement however large the population, provided that it is finite.
elements.sent-2411-003: The problem was previously known to be decidable and EXPTIME-hard.
elements.sent-2411-004: This paper tackles the exact complexity: the problem is EXPTIME-complete.
elements.sent-2411-005: The population control problem was introduced in~\cite{lmcs:5647 in its non-deterministic version, and furthermore studied in~\cite{DBLP:conf/fossacs/ColcombetFO20 in its stochastic version, the \problem.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: The latter is a control problem for a finite population of agents, every agent is a copy of the same finite-state Markov decision process.
elements.sent-2411-007: This finite population is controlled by a central decision-maker, who can select one central action at every discrete time step, and this action applies simultaneously to every member of the population: every MDP is updated to a new state, according to the global action, the local state of the MPD, and the transition probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-008: The goal of the decision maker is to put all the MDPs simultaneously in a final state.
elements.sent-2411-009: Some intuition can be conveyed by using a shepherd-like metaphor.
elements.sent-2411-010: One can depict the finite population as a herd of sheep, and the central decision maker is the shepherd.
elements.sent-2411-011: The shepherd can send his dog to bark here or there, and all the sheeps will perceive this noise, and react accordingly, in the typical randomized way the sheep react to this kind of stimulus.
elements.sent-2411-012: Part of the shepherd job consists in gathering the stray sheep back in the herd, which is doable in finite time as long as the population is finite, but would become too challenging if the herd was infinite, a case we do not consider in the current paper.
elements.sent-2411-014: The former paper provides a decision procedure whose termination relies on well-quasi-order arguments, and lead to non-elementary complexity upper-bounds.
elements.sent-2411-015: The latter paper shows that the problem is at least as hard to solve in its randomized version that it is in its deterministic version.
elements.sent-2411-016: The current paper shows that this lower-complexity bound is optimal: the problem is \exptime-complete (Theorem~\ref{theo:main ).}
elements.sent-2411-017: Section~\ref{sec:model introduces the model and the key ingredients for the decision procedure of~\cite{DBLP:conf/fossacs/ColcombetFO20 .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Section~\ref{sec:counting shows that the shepherd can win by counting up to $|S|$, the number of states in the MDP, independently of the size of the population.}
elements.sent-2411-019: This relies on two playing modes: funnelling obedient sheeps (Section~\ref{sec:luckyplays ) and gathering stray sheeps (Section~\ref{sec:gather ), between which the shepherd can alternate thanks to a key conceptual ingredient: the Isolation Lemma (Section~\ref{ref:isolation ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Finally, in Section~\ref{sec:exptime , we improve the complexity down to \exptime, using data structures introduced in~\cite{lmcs:5647 .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: A controller tries to control a large, yet finite, set of tokens $T_N=1\ldots N$.
elements.sent-2411-023: Each token is a copy of the same Markov Decision Process (MDP) $\mdp=(S,A,\delta : S \times A \to \Delta(S))$.
elements.sent-2411-026: The $N$-population MDP is the synchronized product of $N$ copies of the game.
elements.sent-2411-027: The initial state is $(i,i,...,i) \in S^N$ and the set of final states is $F^N$.
elements.sent-2411-029: The parametrized version where $N$ is arbitrarily large is co-recursively enumerable: it suffices to find one value of $N$ where the answer is negative.
elements.sent-2411-030: Decidability was established in~\cite{DBLP:conf/fossacs/ColcombetFO20 , in the rest of the section we provide the essential ingredients of the decidability proof.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Fix an automaton with a set of states $S$ and a set of final states $F$.
elements.sent-2411-032: A configuration is a vector \begin{equation}w \in S^T\end{equation} which describes the position of every token.
elements.sent-2411-033: A commit is a pair \begin{equation}(v,a)\in S^T \times A\enspace.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-034: If from a commit $v$ there is positive probability to reach some position $w\in S^T$ when playing $a$ then we say $w$ is a successor of $(v,a)$ and denote it \begin{equation}(v,a)\to w\enspace.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-035: We extend the successor relation between commits: $(v,a)\to (w,b)$ iff $(v,a)\to w$.
elements.sent-2411-036: This naturally defines a notion of path between two commits, denoted $(v,a)\to^* (w,b)$.
elements.sent-2411-037: Sometimes, we do not care of the exact state of every token, we only need to pay attention to the number of tokens on every state.
elements.sent-2411-038: An anonymous commit is a pair $(w,a) \in \NN^S\times A$.
elements.sent-2411-039: The anonymous configuration associated with a configuration $w\in S^T$ is the vector: \begin{equation}\left(|\{t \in T \mid w =s \}|\right)_{s\in S} \in \NN^S\enspace.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-040: When clear from the context, we skip the "anonymous" qualificative and simply say a commit and a configuration.
elements.sent-2411-041: No dead-end: every anonymous configuration of $W$ has at least one successor commit in $W$.
elements.sent-2411-042: Stability under chance moves: every anonymous commit of $W$ has all its successors configurations in $W$.
elements.sent-2411-043: We say that a (non-anonymous) configuration or commit $w\in S^T \cup S^T\times A$ belongs to $W$ if the corresponding anonymous configuration or commit does.
elements.sent-2411-044: Sometimes, an arena $W$ is equipped with a target set $W_F \subseteq W$.
elements.sent-2411-045: In that case we relax the definition and we allow configurations in $W_F$ to be dead-ends.
elements.sent-2411-046: A strategy in $W$ is a mapping which associates with every configuration $v\in W$ a probability distribution on the set of actions $a$ such that $(v,a) \in W$.
elements.sent-2411-047: All strategies we consider are positional, which is enough for reachability games.
elements.sent-2411-048: A strategy is state-based if the action chosen only depends on the number of tokens in every state, not the identity of those tokens.
elements.sent-2411-049: The safe random walk is an almost-surely winning strategy.. It is state-based.
elements.sent-2411-050: The goal is to almost-surely put all tokens in $F$.
elements.sent-2411-051: A configuration $w_0$ is almost-surely winning if there is a strategy in $W$ which guarantees almost-surely that any play strating in $w_0$ eventually reaches a final configuration.
elements.sent-2411-052: A commit is almost-surely winning if all its successors are.
elements.sent-2411-054: The set of almost-surely winning configurations and commits is the largest arena $W$ such that from every configuration in $W$ there is a path within $W$ to a final configuration.
elements.sent-2411-055: The upper closure of an (anonymous) commit $(v,a)$ is the set of commits $(w,a)$ such that $w\geq v$, pointwise on all coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-057: However, it can be implemented in finite space and time.
elements.sent-2411-058: We make use of the ordered monoid \begin{equation}\Nb = \{0 < 1<2<3<\ldots< \omega\}\enspace.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-059: Given $N\in \NN$ and a symbolic configuration $w\in \Nb^S$, denote $w[N]$ the configuration were all $\omega$'s are replaced by $N$.
elements.sent-2411-060: A symbolic commit is an element of $\Nb^S\times A$.
elements.sent-2411-061: The ideal generated by a symbolic configuration $\wb$ is denoted $\ideal{\wb}$, it it the set of anonymous configurations whose coordinates are below those of $\wb$: \begin{equation}\ideal{\wb} ~= \{ w \in \NN^S \mid \forall s \in S, w(s) \leq \wb(s)\}\enspace.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-062: The notation is extended to set of commits, by taking the union of the ideals.. \begin{equation}\ideal{\{(\wb_i,a_i), {i\in I}\}} = \bigcup_{i\in I} \ideal{\wb}\enspace.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-063: When $W\subseteq \NN^S$ is a set of anonymous configurations and $\wb\in \Nb^S$ a symbolic configuration, we say that $\wb$ belongs to $W$, denoted $\wb \in W$, whenever \begin{equation}\ideal{\wb} \subseteq W\enspace.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-065: The symbolic initial state is the symbolic configuration \begin{equation}\ib\in \Nb^S\end{equation} where all components are $0$ except for $i$.
elements.sent-2411-066: The symbolic final state is the symbolic configuration \begin{equation}\Fb\in \Nb^S\end{equation} where all components are $0$ except for those in $F$.
elements.sent-2411-067: The \problem\ can be rephrased as follows \fbox{ \begin{minipage {35em {\bf \problem: does there exists a strategy which almost-surely guarantees reaching $\Fb$ when starting the play in $\ib$~? \end{minipage As a consequence.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: The fix-point algorithm maintains as an invariant that $W$ is downward-closed and can be effectively represented as the downward-closure of a finite set of symbolic commits.. The algorithm terminates.
elements.sent-2411-069: The algorithm makes use of two tests in the loop.
elements.sent-2411-070: The first one is quite easy to check.
elements.sent-2411-071: Let $W$ be an arena finitely presented as $W =\ideal{\{(\wb_i,a_i), {i\in 0\ldots n}\}}$.
elements.sent-2411-072: Whether there exists a commit $(v,a)\in W$ from which there is a chance to escape $W$ is decidable.
elements.sent-2411-073: We enumerate all commits $(\wb_i,a_i)$, one by one.
elements.sent-2411-074: Say a flow graph from $\wb$ to $\wb'$ is maximally spreading if for every state $s$ with $\wb(s)=\omega$, all edges from $s$ are labelled by $\omega$\enspace.
elements.sent-2411-075: We generate all possible maximally spreading flow graphs from $\wb_i$ to some symbolic configuration $\wb'_i$\enspace.
elements.sent-2411-077: We check that for every such $\wb'_i$, there exists an action $b$ such that $(\wb'_i,b)\in W$\enspace.
elements.sent-2411-078: The answer is positive iff all checks are successful.
elements.sent-2411-079: The second test can be expressed as the following decision problem.
elements.sent-2411-081: The path decision problem asks whether, from every configuration in $\ideal{\wb}$, there is a path in the arena $W$ to reach $\Fb$.
elements.sent-2411-082: Colcombet et al. could prove that the path problem is decidable.
elements.sent-2411-083: In case the answer is negative, the decision procedure outputs an integer $N$ such that there is no path from $\wb[N]$ to a final configuration in $W$.
elements.sent-2411-084: This leads to a procedure with non elementary complexity, since the best upper-bound to our knowledge on the number of calls to the path problem is non-elementary.
elements.sent-2411-085: It was conjectured~\cite{corto that computing an almost-surely winning strategy does not require such fine description of the winning zone, and that tracking a small number of tokens, plus a set of unbounded states, is enough to solve population MDPs.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Sometimes we will illustrate concepts by associating a symbolic configuration into a shepherd-style metaphor: the tokens are sheep which splits between the herd, which occupies the $\omega$-coordinates and the stray sheep, which occupies the finite coordinates, as illustrated on Figure~\ref{fig:sheep .. \begin{center.}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=10cm]{sheeps.jpg} \caption{\label{fig:sheep} The herd and the stray sheep.}
elements.sent-2411-087: The configurations in $W_{0,\omega}$ are called purely symbolic winning configurations, which always contains $\Fb$.
elements.sent-2411-088: Moreover $W_{0,\omega}$ contains $\ib$ iff the answer to the \problem\ is positive.. Denote \begin{equation}m = |S| \enspace.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-089: The following theorem shows that Corto's conjecture holds.
elements.sent-2411-090: There exists a subarena $Y$ of $W$ such that:.
elements.sent-2411-091: To show this theorem, we make use of almost-surely winning strategies which alternate between two playing modes.
elements.sent-2411-092: The first mode is called ``Funneling obedient sheep''.
elements.sent-2411-093: In this mode the tokens follow a predetermined path, called a funnel path, which leads to a final set.
elements.sent-2411-094: On the funnel path, only a few sheep at a time are allowed to leave the herd, and none of them ever meet: they are expected to stay in different states, and thus there ate most $|S|$ of them at any moment.
elements.sent-2411-095: The controller selects actions to stay on the funnel path, and expect the sheep to follow the predetermined path, which might occur with positive probability, but in general this is not for sure.
elements.sent-2411-096: In case the sheep leave the funnel path, the controller enters the second playing mode.
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{funneling.jpg} \caption{\label{fig:funelling} Funneling obedient sheep} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{gathering.jpg} \caption{ Gathering the herd} \end{subfigure} \caption{\label{fig:gathering} The two modes of the controller}
elements.sent-2411-098: The second mode is called ``Gathering the herd'', it is entered when some non-obedient sheep have left the funnel path.
elements.sent-2411-099: At this moment the sheep that do not belong to the herd are considered as stray sheep.
elements.sent-2411-100: The controller objective is to gather all sheep back together into a herd, including the stray sheep.
elements.sent-2411-101: There is an extra constraint: at every moment, the controller is expected to control the stray sheep so that there at most $m$ of them (at most $|S|$ in one state, at most $|S|-1$ in another state, and so on).
elements.sent-2411-102: Such a play is called a funnel path.
elements.sent-2411-103: The proof of lemma~\ref{lem:luckyplays is given in section~\ref{sec:luckyplays , where a more general statement is proved (Lemma~\ref{lem:luckyplays2 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: A funnel path might be very long, thus very unlucky to occur on first try.
elements.sent-2411-105: It might be that, by chance, this play occurs as planned, in which case the game is over and controller wins.
elements.sent-2411-106: Whenever the chance exits the funnel path, then the new configuration belongs to $W_{0,\ldots ,|S|}$ the strategy switches to a recovery strategy in order to reach back $W_{0,\omega}$.
elements.sent-2411-107: Such a strategy is called a gathering strategy.
elements.sent-2411-108: The proof of lemma~\ref{lem:recstart is given in section~\ref{sec:recstart .}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Combining funnel pathes and gathering strategies is the key to keep a low profile.
elements.sent-2411-110: From any $W_{0,\omega}$ configuration, when playing the safe random walk in $W'$, there is a non-zero chance to follow a funnel path and finitely reach $\Fb$.
elements.sent-2411-111: In case the lucky path is exited, there is probability $1$ to reach $W_{0,\omega}$ again.
elements.sent-2411-112: Since there are finitely many possible configurations reachable from a given initial configuration, the non-zero chances are uniformly bounded from below.
elements.sent-2411-114: The random population control problem can be decided in \expspace.
elements.sent-2411-115: According to Theorem~\ref{thm:cortosconj , the answer to the \problem~is positive iff there exists a downward-closed set $Y$ of configurations and commits generated by a finite family $(w_i,a_i)_{i\in I}$ such that,.}
elements.sent-2411-116: There are at most $(m+2)^{|S| (1 + |A|)$ different possible configurations and commits thus we can assume $|I| \leq (m+2)^{|S| (1 + |A|)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-117: The second condition is easy to check in time polynomial in $|I|$ and $m$, as proved in Lemma~\ref{lem:escape .}
elements.sent-2411-118: The existence of a winning path in $Y$ from any $\{0,\omega\}$-configuration in $Y$, can be performed using a distance automaton of size polynomial in $|I|$, which can be performed in space polynomial in $|I|$ (see~ \cite[Theorem 5.1]{DBLP:conf/fossacs/ColcombetFO20 and~\cite[Theorem~2.2]{DBLP:journals/ita/Kirsten05 for details).}}
elements.sent-2411-119: The isolation lemma is one of the pillars of the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:cortosconj , informally it says that when a group of stray sheep leaves the herd, the stray sheep can be brought back in the herd without ever meeting any other stray sheep outside their group.}
elements.sent-2411-120: Assume that we play in some arena $W$, from some initial position $w_0$.
elements.sent-2411-121: On some states we have a large number of tokens, that altogether form the herd, and on top of that there are also have a few other tokens called the stray sheep.
elements.sent-2411-122: In formal statement, the set of tokens $T_\omega$ typically denotes the herd while $T_1$ typically denotes the stray sheep.
elements.sent-2411-123: In a configuration, we say that a set of states is an $\omega$-base if an arbitrary amount of extra tokens could be placed on these states without exiting the arena $W$.
elements.sent-2411-124: This is formally defined as follows.
elements.sent-2411-125: Let $T_\omega\subseteq T$ be a subset of tokens.
elements.sent-2411-126: We denote \begin{equation}w[{T_\omega *N}]\end{equation} the configuration obtained from $w$ by replacing every token $t\in T_\omega$ with $N$ copies $t_1,t_2,\ldots, t_N$.
elements.sent-2411-127: By extension, if $S_\omega$ is a set of states, and $T_\omega$ is exactly the set of tokens occupying those states in $w$, the configuration $w[{T_\omega *N ]$ is also denoted $w[{S_\omega *N ]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: Of course, we want to duplicate tokens without exiting our arena, which is captured by the notion of $\omega$-base.
elements.sent-2411-129: A set of tokens $T_\omega$ is an $\omega$-base of $w$ in $W$ if \begin{equation}\forall N \in \NN, w[{T_\omega *N}]\in W\enspace.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-130: By extension, a set of states $T_\omega$ is an $\omega$-base of $w$ in $W$ if the set of tokens occupying those states in $w$ is.
elements.sent-2411-131: Dually, a set of tokens $T_1$ is a finite base of $w$ in $W$ iff its complement is an $\omega$-base of $w$ in $W$.
elements.sent-2411-132: Initially the set of states occupied by the herd is unbounded, but later on there might a few sheep escaping the herd and occupying states that are bounded.
elements.sent-2411-133: In a configuration, we say that a group of stray sheep is brought back in the herd whenever the the set of states occupied by the herd and those sheep is unbounded.
elements.sent-2411-134: It might be the case that some states are simultaneously occupied by sheep from the herd and some of the stray sheep, but this is not required in the definition.
elements.sent-2411-135: In a configuration $w$, we say that some stray sheep of $T_1$ are brought back in the herd if there exists a strict subset of $T_1$ which is a finite base of $w$.
elements.sent-2411-136: As already noticed, this definition does not require that stray sheep actually meet some sheep of the herd, i.e. the previous definition does not impose $\exists t \in T_1, t'\in T_\omega, w = w[t']$.
elements.sent-2411-137: The main result of this section is the isolation lemma.
elements.sent-2411-138: Assume that from a configuration where the herd is unbounded, there exists a strategy which almost-surely guarantees to eventually bring back some stray sheep in the herd.
elements.sent-2411-139: Then the same holds with the extra requirement that until some stray sheep are brought back in the herd, all stray sheep stay isolated from the herd, in the sense where they never share a common state.
elements.sent-2411-140: A set of tokens $T_1$ is isolated in a configuration $w$ if it does not meet any token outside $T_1$.
elements.sent-2411-141: Denote $F$ the set of configurations in which some stray sheep of $T_1$ are brought back in the herd.
elements.sent-2411-142: Assume $F$ is almost-surely reachable from everywhere in $W$.
elements.sent-2411-143: Then there is a strategy $\sigma$ in $W$ which, when starting from $w_0$:.
elements.sent-2411-144: It is enough to show that from every configuration $w_0\in W$, there is a path to $F$ on which the stray sheep stay isolated until reaching $F$, and in every successor configuration of every commit of the path, the stray sheep are isolated as well.
elements.sent-2411-145: The strategy consists in trying following this path and in case of exit, try again a new path.
elements.sent-2411-146: That occurs in a finite set of reachable configurations, hence the strategy will eventually succeeds in following the path.
elements.sent-2411-147: Denote $C$ the set of tokens in $w_0$ which are not in $T_1$, and $S_\omega$ the states occupied in $w_0$ by tokens in $C$.
elements.sent-2411-148: By hypothesis, $S_\omega$ is an $\omega$-base of $w_0$ in $W$.
elements.sent-2411-149: Let $M$ be a large constant, and $z_M=w_0[S_\omega * (M+1)]$ the configuration obtained from $w_0$ by duplicating every token of $S_\omega$ into $M+1$ copies, so that we get a larger set of tokens on $S_\omega$, denoted $C_M$, called the herd tokens of $z_M$.
elements.sent-2411-150: We say a token $t$ is blowable in a configuration if $\{t\}$ is an $\omega$-base of this configuration.
elements.sent-2411-151: Denote $F'$ the set of configurations where one of the stray sheep in $T_1$ is blowable.
elements.sent-2411-153: Let $\sigma$ be the random walk in $W$, which guarantees to reach $F$ from everywhere in $W$, a fortiori to reach $F'$.
elements.sent-2411-154: Let $t$ be one of the stray sheep of $T_1$.
elements.sent-2411-155: When $t$ meets one or more herd tokens of $z_M$, we call this event a meeting.
elements.sent-2411-156: Denote $V$ the random variable counting the number of different herd tokens met by $t$ before reaching $F'$.
elements.sent-2411-157: We show that the expected value of $V$ is finite and upper-bounded independently of $M$.
elements.sent-2411-158: Let $B$ be the largest constant used to define $W$ as a finite union of ideals.
elements.sent-2411-159: We argue that the probability that $V$ is above $B\cdot |S| \cdot \ell$ decreases exponentially with $\ell$.
elements.sent-2411-160: First, we show that, every time $t$ meets some herd tokens of $z_M$, there is probability at most $1 - 1/B$ that one of the tokens met by $t$ becomes blowable before $t$ does.
elements.sent-2411-161: Until $F'$ is reached, no more than $B-1$ other tokens can share the same state than $t$.
elements.sent-2411-162: The random walk $\sigma $ is defined with respect to anonymous configurations, like $W$ is.
elements.sent-2411-163: Thus $\sigma$ does not make a difference between $t$ and the herd tokens meeting $t$, thus after a meeting, all of them have the same probability measure on their possible future trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-164: Since $\sigma$ guarantees to almost-surely reach $F$ someday, all of the herd tokens met by $t$ will almost-surely become blowable someday, thus $t$ as well.
elements.sent-2411-165: By symmetry, all of them have the same probability to be the first to become blowable, thus the probability that none of the $\leq B-1$ herd tokens becomes blowable until $t$ does is at least $1/B$.
elements.sent-2411-166: Look at those finite pathes where $t$ meets at least $B|S|$ different herd tokens, and still $t$ is not blowable.
elements.sent-2411-167: By definition of $B$, among those $B|S|$ herd tokens, at least $1$ has became blowable.
elements.sent-2411-168: Thus, at every moment the probability that $t$ meets $B|S|$ different herd tokens in the future is at most $1 - 1/B$, hence \begin{equation}\mathbb{P}_{\sigma,z_M}(V \geq B\cdot |S| \cdot \ell) \leq (1 - 1/B)^\ell\enspace.\end{equation}. So finally \begin{equation}\label{eq:nbencountersbounded} \mathbb{E}_{\sigma,z_M}[V] \leq \sum_{\ell} B\cdot |S| \cdot (\ell+1) \cdot (1 - 1/B)^\ell = K(B,|S|)\enspace.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-169: Remark that the right handside of~\eqref{eq:nbencountersbounded is an upper-bound independent of $M$.}
elements.sent-2411-170: We deduce that for every herd token in $Z_M$, the probability that this herd token in particular meets $t$ before $t$ becomes blowable converges to $0$ when $M$ grows.
elements.sent-2411-171: Let $s$ be one of the states on which the herd tokens are placed in $w_0$.
elements.sent-2411-172: There are at least $M$ herd tokens of $C_M$ on $s$.
elements.sent-2411-173: Since $\sigma_M$ is state based, all of them have the same probability $p_{t,s,M}$ to meet $t$ before reaching $F'$.
elements.sent-2411-174: According to~\eqref{eq:nbencountersbounded , \begin{equation}\label{eq:probaencountertozero} p_{t,s,M} \leq K(B,|S|) / M \to_M 0\enspace,\end{equation} which converges to $0$ when $M$ grows.}
elements.sent-2411-175: Recall that $C$ denotes the set of tokens in $w_0$ which are not in $T_1$ and $C_M$ their $M$ copies in $z_M$.
elements.sent-2411-176: The strategy $\sigma_M$ from $z_M$ can be projected onto a strategy from $w_0$ by simulating the $M$ copies of every herd token in $C_M\setminus C$.
elements.sent-2411-178: According to~\eqref{eq:probaencountertozero , the probability that a token in $C$ meets a token in $T_1$ before $F'$ occurs can be made arbitrarily small, by augmenting the number $M$ of tokens being simulated, while guaranteeing at the same that $F'$ occurs almost-surely.}
elements.sent-2411-179: Since the set of configurations reachable from $w_0$ is finite, and the corresponding condition is a reachability condition under safety constraint, the probability can be turned to $0$, i.e. $F'$ can be reached almost-surely, while keeping the tokens in $T_1$ isolated.
elements.sent-2411-180: We have not quite proved yet our goal: when starting the play in $w_0$ we can keep the tokens in $T_1$ isolated and reach almost-surely $F'$, but what about reaching $F$? The inclusion $F\subseteq F'$ might be strict because when one of the stray sheep $t$ in $T_1$ become blowable, it might be that other sheep from the herd prevent a strict subset of $T_1\setminus\{t\}$ to be a finite base.
elements.sent-2411-181: However by definition of $B$, by deleting at most $B|S|$ sheep from the herd, we can allow $T_1\setminus\{t\}$ to be a finite base.
elements.sent-2411-182: Call those sheep to be deleted the blockers.
elements.sent-2411-183: Then the expected number of blockers is $\leq B|S|$.
elements.sent-2411-184: This is true not only when the play starts from $w_0$ but also when it starts from $z_M$, because $T_1$ is also a finite base of $z_M$.
elements.sent-2411-185: But then the probability that a given sheep from the herd becomes a blocker is less than $B|S| / M$.
elements.sent-2411-186: Using the same argument than in the previous paragraph, it prove that from $w_0$ we can make the probability that a token in $C$ is a blocker as close to $0$ as we want, while ensuring reaching $F'$ and keeping the tokens in $T_1$ isolated.
elements.sent-2411-187: Since the set of configurations reachable from $w_0$ in $W$ is a finite MDP, we can make this probability $0$, in which case reaching $F'$ is equivalent to reaching $F$.

elements.sent-2411-189: Informally, in this section is exposed the way to guide a herd of obedient sheep to the objective.

elements.subsection-2411-008: Equivalence of the game and the problem
elements.sent-2411-293: This section is dedicated to the proof of the following lemma.
elements.sent-2411-294: Eve wins the game iff the answer to the \problemm\ is positive.
elements.sent-2411-295: One direction is easy: if Eve wins the abstract game then for every $N$ she uses her strategy in the $N$-game to generate an over-approximation of the $N$-play, and uses Adam moves to adjust the over-approximation such that it is tight, i.e. at every moment the play is over-approximated by a minimal abstract configuration.
elements.sent-2411-296: That guarantees the finite capacity constraint is satisfied, because a violation of the finite capacity constraint in the tight over-approximation would imply a violation in the actual $N$-play, which is impossible, cf~\cite[Lemma 3.5]{lmcs:5647 .}
elements.sent-2411-297: For the converse implication, use the fact that if the answer to the \problemm\ is positive then the corresponding distance automata build in~\cite{DBLP:conf/fossacs/ColcombetFO20 on the alphabet of symbolic $\{0,1,\omega\}$-flow is unbounded.}
elements.sent-2411-298: According to~\cite[Theorem 12]{simontropical , there exists a $\sharp$-expression $e$ which witness unboundedness: the cost of the sequence of words $(e[n])_n$ is unbounded, where $e[n]$ denotes the expression obtained by replacing $\sharp$ with $n$.}
elements.sent-2411-299: Such $\sharp$-expression $e$ is called a $\sharp$-path from $\wb_0$ to $\Wb$, where $\wb_0$ is an $\{0,1,\omega\}$-configuration generating the initial configurations ideal, and $\Wb$ is the set of final configurations.
elements.sent-2411-300: Using the dictionary provided by~\cite{DBLP:conf/fossacs/ColcombetFO20 , it means the maximal number $k_N$ of tokens that can be moved from $\wb_0[N]$ to $\ideal{\Wb}$ through the capacity graph formed by the sequence of symbolic matrices $e[N]$ is unbounded when $N$ grows large.}
elements.sent-2411-301: The proof is by induction on the structure of the $\sharp$-expression, with induction hypothesis:.
elements.sent-2411-302: There are four cases, corresponding to the two operators (concatenation and iteration), plus the base case.
elements.sent-2411-304: If the $\sharp$-path from $\wb_0$ to $\Wb$ is a single letter $a$, then a winning strategy for Eve consists in playing $a$.
elements.sent-2411-305: If the $\sharp$-path from $\wb_0$ to $\Wb$ is a product $e \cdot f$, for every integer $N$ denote $k_N$ the maximal number of tokens that can be moved along the capacity graph $e[N] f[N]$ and $\pi_N$ the corresponding trajectory of the $k_N$ tokens, and $w'_N$ the configuration on $\pi_N$ reached at the end of $e[N]$ (hence at the beginning of $f[N]$).
elements.sent-2411-306: Denote $\wb$ a limit in the topological closure of $\{w'_N \mid N\}$ and set $\Wb'=\{\wb\}$.
elements.sent-2411-307: Then $e$ is a witness of $\wb_0 \to \{\wb\}$ and $f$ is a witness of $\wb \to \Wb$.
elements.sent-2411-308: Apply the induction hypotheses to $e$ and $f$ to get two strategies for Eve, $\sigma_e$ and $\sigma_f$, which win in the corresponding games.
elements.sent-2411-309: Then a winning strategy for Eve consists in playing $\sigma_e$ and then as soon a configuration $\leq \wb$ is reached, switch to $\sigma_f$, until $\Wb$ is reached.
elements.sent-2411-310: If the $\sharp$-path from $\wb_0$ to $\Wb$ is an iteration $e^\sharp$, then the corresponding capacity graph $e$ is idempotent, thus its image $\wb$ is contained in its domain $\wb_0$.
elements.sent-2411-311: If $e=e^\sharp$ (i.e. $e$ is stable), then also $e$ is a sharp path from $\wb_0$ to $\Wb$, and we conclude by indction hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-312: If $e\neq e^\sharp$ (i.e. $e$ is unstable) Eve can play again and again the strategy $\sigma_e$ associated with $e$, to go from $\wb_0$ to $\wb \leq \wb_0$.
elements.sent-2411-313: Each time $\wb$ is reached, Eve reboots the strategy $\sigma_e$ from scratch $\wb_0$.
elements.sent-2411-314: We need to add a special dummy letter which is used to force a move from Adam to handle the possible strict inequality $\wb \leq \wb_0$.
elements.sent-2411-315: Since the idempotent is unstable, Adam must either violate the finite capacity constraint, or eventually empty any connected component which is not minimam in the topological order (i.e. a BSCC).
elements.sent-2411-316: In both cases, Eve eventually wins.
elements.sent-2411-317: The game can be solved in EXPTIME.
elements.sent-2411-318: The winning condition can be recognised by a parity automaton of exponential size and bounded number of colors~\cite[Section 4.3]{lmcs:5647.}
elements.sent-2411-319: By combining Lemma~\ref{lem:compreduce and Lemma~\ref{lem:lpp we obtain our main result.}}
elements.sent-2411-321: Next step is solving population parity games, which we conjecture are also in \exptime.
elements.sent-2411-322: The problem was previously known to be decidable and \exptime-hard.
elements.sent-2411-323: This paper tackles the exact complexity: the problem is \exptime-complete.
elements.sent-2411-324: According to~\cite{mascle2019controllingrandompopulationexptimehard , we cannot hope better than \exptime.}
elements.sent-2411-325: So finally the \exptime upper bound reduces to the proof of decidability of the \problemm\ with largest constant $1$, which is dne in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-326: The game can be solved in \exptime.
elements.sent-2411-327: The population control problem is a parameterized control problem where a population of agents has to be moved simultaneously into a target state.
elements.sent-2411-328: The decision problem asks whether this can be achieved for an finite but arbitrarily large population.
elements.sent-2411-329: We focus on the random version of this problem, where every agent is a copy of the same automaton and non-determinism on the {global action chosen by the controller is resolved independently and uniformly at random.}
elements.sent-2411-330: Controller seeks to almost-surely gather the agents in the target states.
elements.sent-2411-331: We show that the random population control problem is \exptime-complete.
elements.sent-2411-332: An arbitrary number $N$ of tokens are placed on the initial state of a non-deterministic finite automaton.
elements.sent-2411-333: They are updated in rounds, each resolving their non-deterministic choices: in every round, a controller selects a letter $a$ and then every token independently selects and moves to an $a$-labelled successor state to the one it resides in.
elements.sent-2411-334: The goal for the controller is to eventually gather all tokens in an accepting state.
elements.sent-2411-335: The larger the number $N$, the harder it is to control the population of $N$ tokens.
elements.sent-2411-336: Crucially, the decision question we study is parameterized in the number of tokens: given the automaton, decide whether controller can succeed for arbitrarily large population sizes $N$? This approach follows a line of models for biological or chemical systems with large crowds of simple finite-state systems, like population protocols~\cite{AngluinADFP06 or Petri nets~\cite{Koch10 .}}
elements.sent-2411-337: Population control problems provide a formal framework for the design of strategies to control a large number of identical agents.
elements.sent-2411-338: This framework was introduced in~\cite{lmcs:5647 , as a model for the synchronization of large populations of yeasts~\cite{UhlendorfMDCFBHB15 .}}
elements.sent-2411-339: It fits into parameterized verification, a line of research that aims to verify distributed protocols over arbitrarily large networks~\cite{SistlaG87,EmersonN95 .}
elements.sent-2411-340: The population control problem has been studied both in the adversarial and stochastic settings, which differ in how agents resolve choices and what guarantees the controller is after.
elements.sent-2411-341: In the adversarial setting, an antagonistic environment resolves all agent's choices, trying to avoid synchronization.
elements.sent-2411-342: Bertrand et al.~\cite{BertrandDGG17,lmcs:5647 showed that this (adversarial) population control problem is decidable and \exptime-complete.}
elements.sent-2411-343: In the stochastic setting, all agent's choices are made uniformly at random and the controller aims to synchronize the agents almost-surely, with probability one.
elements.sent-2411-344: This random population control is known to be \exptime-hard \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1909-06420 and decidable \cite{DBLP:conf/fossacs/ColcombetFO20,ColcombetFO21 .}}
elements.sent-2411-345: Colcombet et al.'s decision procedure is based on two key ingredients.
elements.sent-2411-346: First, winning regions are downward-closed with respect to the natural product order on $\NN^S$ and can therefore be finitely represented and manipulated as a union of ideals.
elements.sent-2411-347: The second key ingredient involves the sequential flow problem, which asks whether an unbounded number of tokens can be moved from one set of states to another while remaining within a specific safe region.
elements.sent-2411-348: They solve this problem by a reduction to the boundedness problem on distance automata \cite{DBLP:journals/ita/Kirsten05 .}
elements.sent-2411-349: The main limitation of this approach is that the termination of the resulting algorithm relies on well quasi-orders, resulting in a non-primitive recursive upper bound.
elements.sent-2411-350: We show that the random population control problem is \exptime-complete (Theorem~\ref{thm:main ).}
elements.sent-2411-351: The upper-bound proceeds in two steps, first arguing that it is sufficient for successful strategies to be based on a bounded abstraction of the winning regions (Theorem~\ref{thm:cortosconj ).}
elements.sent-2411-352: The second step is to solve what we call the \problemm in exponential time.
elements.sent-2411-353: Roughly speaking, this problem asks whether we can transfer, with positive probability, arbitrarily many tokens from an initial configuration to the set of final configurations, and all the while surely staying in a given set of safe configurations.
elements.sent-2411-354: To solve the \problemm we compute an appropriate semigroup, called the flow semigroup, which characterizes the existence of such unbounded paths (Theorem~\ref{thm:problem-one ).}
elements.sent-2411-355: To prove this characterization correct, we use two intermediate semigroups called the cut semigroups, one infinite and the other of doubly-exponential size (Theorem~\ref{thm:eqq ).}
elements.sent-2411-356: We exhibit a duality between the flow semigroup and the symbolic cut semigroup which is a reflection of the max-flow min-cut duality (Lemma~\ref{lem:duality ).}
elements.sent-2411-357: This contribution also contains the original (unpublished) lower bound \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1909-06420 (Theorem~\ref{thm:exp-hard ).}}
elements.sent-2411-358: Occurrences of some "notions" are linked to their ""definition"".
elements.sent-2411-359: The reader can click on words and symbols (or just hover over them on some PDF readers) to see the definition.
elements.sent-2411-360: We assume familiarity with automata theory \cite{rozenberg1997handbook and Markov Decision Processes \cite{Puterman:book and proceed to recall some necessary notation.}}
elements.sent-2411-361: We consider MDPs with almost-sure reachability objectives, given by a set $F\subseteq \states$ of target states.
elements.sent-2411-362: When $F$ is reached the play is over and controller wins.
elements.sent-2411-363: If the play is infinite then controller loses.
elements.sent-2411-364: For such objectives it is well-known~\cite[Thm.~B]{Ornstein:AMS1969 that stationary (a.k.a.~memoryless) strategies suffice to win.}
elements.sent-2411-365: A strategy might be partially defined, typically it is not defined on states from which there is no hope to win.
elements.sent-2411-366: If a state on which $\sigma$ is not defined is reached, and if moreover this state is not final, then the play is over and controller loses.
elements.sent-2411-367: Once a strategy $\sigma$ is fixed, the resulting process is a Markov chain, whose probability measure is denoted $\mathbb{P _\sigma$ (see~\cite{Puterman:book for details).}}
elements.sent-2411-368: A strategy is ""winning@@strat"" from some state if it ensures that $F$ is reached $\mathbb{P _\sigma$-almost-surely.}
elements.sent-2411-369: In an MDP with finitely many states, there is a canonical almost-surely winning strategy for Controller: play at random any action which guarantees to stay in the winning region.
elements.sent-2411-370: This is formalized using arenas and safe random walks, as follows.
elements.sent-2411-372: An ""arena"" is a set $W \subseteq \states \times \act$ of commits such that for all $(w,a) \in W$ and $s \in \states$, if $\prob(w,a)(s) > 0$ then there exists $b \in \act$ such that $(s,b) \in W$.
elements.sent-2411-373: We occasionally abuse notations and write $s \in W$ instead of $\exists b \in \act, (s,b) \in W$.
elements.sent-2411-374: The ""safe random walk"" in the "arena" $W$ is the strategy $\sigma$ defined on $W$ such that $\sigma(w)$ is the uniform distribution on $\{a\in\act \mid (w,a) \in W\}$.
elements.sent-2411-375: An "arena" is ""winning@@arena"" if for all $w \in W$ there is a path in $W$ from $w$ to $F$.
elements.sent-2411-376: This is closely linked with the notion of a "winning region", as shown in the next lemma.
elements.sent-2411-377: The union of two winning "arenas" is a winning arena and the winning region is the projection on $\states$ of the largest winning arena.
elements.sent-2411-378: In a winning arena, the safe random walk is a winning strategy from every state.
elements.sent-2411-379: We consider populations of random agents, called tokens, described by finite automata: for an NFA $\A = (\states, \act, \delta)$, let $\?M(\A)=(\states, \act, \prob)$ denote the MDP that resolves non-deterministic choices uniformly at random.
elements.sent-2411-380: The $n$-fold product of $\?M(\A)$ is the MDP where the controller selects at each step a global action $a\in \act$ which applies simultaneously to all $n$ tokens, whose states are independently updated with respect to the global action.
elements.sent-2411-381: Formally, $\?M(\A)^{n =(\states^{n ,\act,\prob)$ is the MDP whose states, called configurations here, are $n$-dimensional vectors with components in $\states$, and $\prob$ is lifted to $\states^{n}$ in the natural way: $\prob(\vec{q ,a)(\vec{p )=\prod_{i=0 ^{n-1}\prob(\vec{q (i),a)(\vec{p (i))$ for all $\vec{q ,\vec{p}\in \states^{n}$ and $a\in\act$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-382: The configuration of $M(\A)^{n}$ where a state $i\in S$ is duplicated $n$ times is denoted $i^{(n)}$.

elements.sent-2411-385: The decision problem asks whether this can be achieved for a finite but arbitrarily large population.
elements.sent-2411-386: We consider MDPs with almost-sure reachability objectives, given by a set $F\subseteq \states$ of target states~\footnote{Exact transition probabilities do not matter for these objectives, so one can assume w.l.o.g.}

elements.sent-2411-389: The $n$-fold product of $\?M$ is the MDP where the controller selects at each step a global action $a\in \act$ which applies simultaneously to all $n$ tokens, whose states are independently updated with respect to the global action.
